text.title: Kubernetes Ingress Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed in K8s Ingress deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Rate-limit annotation `haproxy.org/rate-limit-requests: "60"` is set on an Ingress, but rate limiting never kicks in. The cluster actually runs `ingress-nginx`.
antipattern.root-cause: Author copied the annotation from a different controller's docs without checking which controller is installed.
antipattern.fix: Use `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rpm: "60"` for ingress-nginx. Add a pre-commit linter that asserts every annotation namespace matches the installed controller.

antipattern.symptom: Cluster upgrade drains the controller node; the single Ingress pod is evicted; every HTTP route 502s for 90 s while the replacement comes up.
antipattern.root-cause: Controller Deployment has `replicas: 1` and no PDB.
antipattern.fix: Set `replicas: 3` + PDB `minAvailable: 2`. Add anti-affinity + topology-spread. Drain test: cordon a node, watch all routes stay green.

antipattern.symptom: Cluster has 3 Ingress controller pods, but during a node failure all three vanish — they happened to land on one node.
antipattern.root-cause: No `podAntiAffinity` with `topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname`. The scheduler placed all 3 replicas on the cheapest node.
antipattern.fix: Add `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` anti-affinity on `app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx`. Re-roll the Deployment; pods spread to 3 distinct nodes.
